spec_match.rationale: The CSS-anchor-position-2 spec defines this as a CSS-only effect via @container anchored(--name) — but the script simulates the flip by listening for button clicks and applying a className, not by gating styles on `@container anchored(...)`. The visual outcome looks right but the page doesn't actually exercise the new container-query syntax.

interactive.rationale: Four side buttons toggle the tooltip orientation and the arrow flips visually. Hands-on, not a static card.

blurb_alignment.rationale: Blurb says 'click each fallback to see the arrow rotate via @container anchored(--name)' — the page does the rotate but via JS-applied class names, not the spec's CSS feature. Blurb promises more than the page exercises.

edge_cases.rationale: Four orientations covered; never demonstrates `flip-block`/`flip-inline` combined fallbacks, RTL flow, or what happens when no fallback fits (which is the real motivation for the container query).

design_system.rationale: All colours from CSS variables, no raw hex captured, standard button overrides.

openQuestions.title: Wire the arrow rotation to a real @container anchored(--name) rule
openQuestions.detail: Replace the JS className toggle with the actual spec syntax: set position-try-options + container-type: anchored on the popover, then use `@container anchored(--top) { .arrow { transform: rotate(180deg); } }` etc. The page should fall back to JS only when CSS.supports('container-type: anchored') is false. As written it teaches a JS pattern that mimics the spec rather than the spec itself.

openQuestions.title: Add a feature-detect probe and origin-trial banner
openQuestions.detail: @container anchored(--name) is brand new in v143 — visitors on older browsers will see the JS simulation and assume the CSS feature is what they're seeing. Add a CSS.supports probe at the top with a banner explaining when the page is using the real CSS vs the JS fallback.

openQuestions.title: Cover the flip-block + flip-inline combined case
openQuestions.detail: The four single-axis flips are the easy demo; the harder, more useful case is the combined `flip-block flip-inline` where the tooltip jumps corner-to-corner. Adding a fifth/sixth button for these would push the demo past the spec's headline example.

openQuestions.title: Demonstrate the RTL direction case
openQuestions.detail: anchored() container queries also expose the flow direction; in RTL the arrow rotation for `left` and `right` should swap meaning. A direction toggle would make this visible and would surface a real i18n pitfall.

summary: Looks like the spec's headline demo, but it actually simulates the flip in JS rather than using the new @container anchored(--name) CSS. Rewrite to use the real CSS path with a JS fallback for older browsers — that's the spec_match fix that turns this from 'pattern illustration' into 'feature demo'.
